title: Splitting an Entity Vertically
breadcrumbs: Design Considerations for Performance and Scalability
text: Splitting an Entity Vertically Defining separate entities for subsets of columns that are part of the same entity can boost performance in some cases. This is true in situations where the following conditions are satisfied: The size of a single row (expressed as a number of bytes) of a given entity exceeds the size of one or more data buffers that play a role when retrieving and modifying a row The entity consists of several distinct subsets of columns and each subset is accessed independently of another subset Manipulating rows whose size exceeds the size of specific data buffers results in extra I/O, thereby decreasing performance. The aim of the vertical split is to divide the entity into a subset of columns that are frequently accessed and fit within the relevant data buffers, and a subset that is less frequently accessed. Take, for example, select cache, a Uniface data buffer. If an individual occurrence contains less than a certain amount of data, Uniface uses the select cache mechanism to improve I/O performance. The precise amount of data is governed by the $MAX_CACHE_SIZE assignment setting and cannot exceed 512 bytes. (The exact number might be different, depending on the operating system.) If the size is less than the maximum, complete occurrences are placed in the select cache rather than the primary key or a physical record address. To take full advantage of the select cache mechanism, it can be useful to divide a large entity into smaller entities with a one-to-one or one-to-many relationship between them. The first entity should contain all fields that are referenced regularly; one occurrence of this new entity should not contain more than 512 bytes. The second entity should contain the remaining fields that are not frequently referenced, as shown in the following illustration: Figure 1. Splitting an Entity Vertically Was this topic helpful?
